title: Spain
subtitle: 1900–1975 · Crisis, Republic, Civil War and Dictatorship

categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: Monarchy, Republic, military coup and dictatorship

entries.label: Alfonso XIII — the last Restoration king
entries.hint: Alfonso XIII reigns through colonial collapse, the 1923 military coup, and growing republican pressure — he goes into exile on 14 April 1931 after republican parties win local elections, interpreted as a referendum against the monarchy.
entries.note: Alfonso XIII's abdication letter (14 Apr 1931): published in full in La Gaceta de Madrid (official gazette) and major newspapers of the day. The 1931 local election results — the trigger for his departure — are in the Archivo Histórico Nacional (AHN). Preston (2004), Juan Carlos: A People's King, ch.1 for context.

entries.label: Primo de Rivera — dictatorship that doomed the monarchy
entries.hint: General Miguel Primo de Rivera pronounces in Barcelona on 13 September 1923 — Alfonso XIII accepts him as prime minister; the dictatorship suspends the 1876 Constitution, dissolves the Cortes, bans political parties and establishes a corporate state; its economic success (1920s boom) and political failure (it collapses in 1930) leaves the monarchy compromised and directly causes the 1931 Republic.
entries.note: Primo de Rivera's pronunciamiento 13 Sep 1923: documented in press and official records. Alfonso XIII's acceptance: documented — the king's complicity permanently damages his legitimacy. The suspension of the Constitution: Gaceta de Madrid. The collapse 28 Jan 1930: documented. Ben-Ami (1983), Fascism from Above: The Dictatorship of Primo de Rivera in Spain: the standard English study. The connection to 1931: Tusell (1988) documents how the dictatorship's collapse made the monarchy indefensible.

entries.label: Second Spanish Republic proclaimed
entries.hint: The Second Republic is proclaimed from the balcony of the Interior Ministry at the Puerta del Sol — a jubilant popular moment; the Republic will govern through four phases before the Civil War destroys it.
entries.note: Proclamation 14 April 1931: La Gaceta de Madrid, 15 Apr 1931 — the official gazette records the Provisional Government. Photographs: Agencia EFE archive. The Provisional Government's composition: documented in the same gazette. Carr (1982), Spain 1808–1975: the standard English-language history of the Republic.

entries.label: Manuel Azaña — the Republic's indispensable man
entries.hint: Manuel Azaña is the dominant intellectual-politician of the Second Republic — Prime Minister twice (1931–1933, 1936) and President (1936–1939); his speeches are primary sources for Republican constitutional thought; his diaries (smuggled out to France) are the finest literary document of the Civil War; he dies in exile in Montauban, November 1940.
entries.note: Azaña's diaries (1931–1939): published as Memorias políticas y de guerra — BNE; the primary literary-political document of the Republic. His 'Una política' speech (1930) and the Cortes debates: Diario de Sesiones del Congreso de los Diputados 1931–1933 (digitised, Congreso). Azaña's death Montauban 3 Nov 1940: documented in French civil records. Juliá (2008), Vida y tiempo de Manuel Azaña: the standard biography. His La velada en Benicarló (1939): the most direct literary account of the Republic's collapse.

entries.label: José Antonio Primo de Rivera — the Falange and Spanish fascism
entries.loc: Madrid (Teatro de la Comedia, founding speech); Alicante (executed)
entries.hint: José Antonio Primo de Rivera (son of the dictator) founds the Falange Española at the Teatro de la Comedia on 29 October 1933 — the founding speech is the primary document of Spanish fascism; arrested by the Republic in 1936, he is executed in Alicante on 20 November 1936 (the same day Franco enters Madrid); Franco appropriates his movement and mythology.
entries.note: The founding speech 29 Oct 1933: ABC 30 Oct 1933 (BNE Hemeroteca) — published verbatim. José Antonio's execution Alicante 20 Nov 1936: documented in Republican military tribunal records (AHN). The Falange programme (27 points, 1934): BNE — the primary ideological document. Preston (1994), Franco: A Biography, ch.5 analyses the José Antonio–Franco relationship. The body's transfer to the Valle de los Caídos (1959): documented — the cult of the 'absent one' (el ausente). Rodríguez Jiménez (2000), Historia de Falange Española de las JONS: the standard study.

entries.label: Franco's coup and the military uprising
entries.hint: The military uprising begins in Spanish Morocco on 17 July 1936 — within hours Franco broadcasts his manifesto; the coup fails to take Madrid and Barcelona immediately, triggering three years of civil war rather than a quick seizure of power.
entries.note: Franco's manifesto (18 Jul 1936): text published in Diario de Burgos and preserved in AHN. The telegram from Melilla beginning the uprising: documented in military archives. Thomas (1961), The Spanish Civil War: the foundational English-language study. Jackson (1965), The Spanish Republic and the Civil War.

entries.label: Franco declared Generalísimo and head of state
entries.hint: The Nationalist junta elevates Franco to supreme command and head of government — the decree is published in the official Nationalist bulletin; his consolidation of personal power over rival generals begins immediately.
entries.note: Boletín Oficial de la Junta de Defensa Nacional, 1 Oct 1936: the decree text. The internal politics of the elevation: Preston (1993), Franco: A Biography, ch.10. The elimination of rival Nationalist leaders (Mola, José Antonio Primo de Rivera) consolidates Franco's position through 1937.

entries.label: Franco — 36 years of dictatorship
entries.hint: Francisco Franco rules Spain from April 1939 to his death on 20 November 1975 — the longest dictatorship in 20th-century Western Europe; his regime archives are partly in the AHN and partly still classified.
entries.note: Franco's death certificate 20 Nov 1975: registered in Madrid civil registry. The Valle de los Caídos decree (Apr 1940): BOE. Franco's 36 years of rule: documented in Boletín Oficial del Estado (BOE), the official state gazette, throughout. Preston (1993), Franco: A Biography: the standard modern biography (900pp).

entries.label: Dolores Ibarruri 'La Pasionaria' — Communist leader and voice of the Republic
entries.hint: Dolores Ibarruri 'La Pasionaria' is the most internationally recognised voice of the Spanish Republic — her 'No pasaran' broadcast (Radio Madrid, 19 July 1936) becomes the Republic's defining slogan; as Communist Party leader she is a figure of enormous symbolic power; she spends 38 years in Soviet exile and returns to Spain in 1977 to be re-elected to parliament at age 81.
entries.note: The 'No pasaran' broadcast 19 Jul 1936: Radio Madrid -- transcribed in the Republican press (Mundo Obrero, 20 Jul 1936, BNE). Ibarruri's memoir El unico camino (1962): primary source. Comintern records on the Spanish Communist Party (PCE): RGASPI (Moscow), fond 495 -- partially declassified. Preston (1999), Comrades: chapters on the Republican leadership. Her return to Madrid 13 May 1977: documented in press. Re-elected to Congreso de los Diputados June 1977: Ministerio del Interior election records.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: Civil war, international intervention and Francoist repression

entries.label: Semana Trágica — Barcelona burns, Ferrer Guardia executed
entries.hint: Conscription of working-class men for the Second Melilla War sparks a general strike and street fighting in Barcelona — over 100 churches and convents burned; the military suppression is brutal; the execution of the anarchist educator Francisco Ferrer Guardia causes international outrage, brings down the Conservative government of Maura, and permanently radicalises the Catalan working class.
entries.note: The general strike declaration 26 Jul 1909: documented in press (La Vanguardia, El Imparcial — BNE Gallica equivalent). The church burnings: documented in police and municipal records (Arxiu Municipal de Barcelona). Ferrer Guardia's trial and execution 13 Oct 1909: documented — his trial records in AHN. The international protest: documented in French, British and German press. Connelly Ullman (1968), The Tragic Week: the standard English study. Maura's government resignation: Gaceta de Madrid. The Semana Trágica permanently ends the possibility of peaceful integration of Barcelona's labour movement.

entries.label: Rif War and Battle of Alhucemas — Spain's colonial trauma
entries.loc: Rif mountains, Morocco; Alhucemas Bay (landing 8 Sep 1925)
entries.hint: The Rif War (1921–1927) follows the Disaster of Annual — Abd el-Krim's Rif Republic holds Spain at bay for six years; the Battle of Alhucemas (8 September 1925) is the decisive turning point: a Spanish-French amphibious landing, the first successful large-scale amphibious operation in modern warfare, planned by Franco and Primo de Rivera; Abd el-Krim surrenders May 1926.
entries.note: The Alhucemas landing 8 Sep 1925: Archivo General Militar (Ávila) — the planning documents. Franco's role: his memoir Diario de una Bandera (1922) and subsequent accounts. Abd el-Krim's memoirs (published 1927): the primary Rif perspective. Shannon Fleming (1991), Primo de Rivera and Abd-el-Krim: the standard English study. The landing's military significance: the template cited by Eisenhower's planners for Operation Torch (1942) — documented. Abd el-Krim's surrender May 1926: documented in French colonial records (ANOM).

entries.label: Asturias uprising — Franco's army rehearsal for the Civil War
entries.hint: The entry of the right-wing CEDA into the cabinet triggers a general strike that becomes a full-scale armed workers' uprising in Asturias — the Socialist and anarchist miners hold out for two weeks; the centre-right government sends the Army of Africa under General Francisco Franco to crush it; thousands killed, 30,000 imprisoned; the direct ideological and military rehearsal for the Civil War.
entries.note: The uprising: documented in press (El Socialista, ABC — BNE). Franco's role as coordinator of the suppression: documented in military records (Archivo General Militar, Ávila). The casualties: approximately 1,500 killed, 30,000 imprisoned — documented in Republican parliamentary debates. Payne (1970), The Spanish Revolution ch.7: the standard study. The Asturias uprising polarises the Republic into two irreconcilable blocs — explicitly acknowledged by all participants. The Army of Africa's deployment against Spanish citizens is the template for July 1936.

entries.label: Military uprising begins — 17 July 1936
entries.hint: The uprising starts in Spanish Morocco — Franco flies from the Canary Islands in a chartered British plane to take command; within 48 hours Spain is divided between Republican government and Nationalist rebels.
entries.note: Franco's flight in the Dragon Rapide: documented in the plane's manifest (preserved). The uprising's spread across garrisons: documented in military and newspaper archives from both sides. Thomas (1961), The Spanish Civil War, Part I. The Dragon Rapide charter: Rankin (2003), Telegram from Guernica.

entries.label: Non-Intervention and the International Brigades
entries.loc: Madrid; Jarama; Teruel (main IB engagement sites)
entries.hint: The Non-Intervention Agreement (27 powers) allows Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy to support Franco while nominally maintaining neutrality — the International Brigades (35,000 volunteers from 50 countries) fight for the Republic; their records survive in the ALBA archive.
entries.note: Non-Intervention Agreement 1936: text in League of Nations archives, Geneva. ALBA (Abraham Lincoln Brigade Archives): digital collection at UCSD — 150+ letters, photographs, personnel records. British Trades Union Congress archive: 46 files on the Civil War (IHR, London). Orwell, Homage to Catalonia (1938): eyewitness by a participant — first edition British Library.

entries.label: White Terror — Francoist mass killings 1936–1945
entries.loc: Spain-wide; mass graves throughout Andalusia, Extremadura, Castile
entries.hint: Francoist forces systematically execute Republican soldiers, officials and civilians during and after the Civil War — the Historical Memory Law (2007) initiated a mass graves programme; current estimates range from 100,000 to 200,000 killed in political repression.
entries.note: UNCERTAIN: Casualty figures disputed. Centro Documental de la Memoria Histórica (Salamanca): the primary archive. Mass graves database: Ministerio de la Presidencia, online from 2007. Preston (2012), The Spanish Holocaust: the most comprehensive modern study, with detailed regional evidence. The 'pact of silence' during the Transition limited documentation; ongoing excavations continue to revise numbers.

entries.label: Siege of Madrid — 'No pasarán'
entries.hint: The Nationalist assault on Madrid begins on 7 November 1936 and fails — 'No pasarán' becomes the Republic's rallying cry; Madrid holds for 28 months against bombardment and siege; the International Brigades' first major action is here (Battle of Jarama, 1937); Madrid does not fall until 28 March 1939 when Casado's coup surrenders the city.
entries.note: The assault of 7 Nov 1936: Archivo General Militar (Ávila) and Soviet military archives (partial access). Dolores Ibárruri's '¡No pasarán!' broadcast: Radio Madrid 19 Jul 1936 — documented; the phrase becomes the Republican emblem. The Battle of Jarama (Feb 1937): documented in International Brigade records (RGASPI, Moscow). Beevor (1982), The Battle for Spain, ch.9–11: the standard account. Preston (2006), The Spanish Civil War, ch.8. The final surrender 28 Mar 1939: Casado's negotiations documented in AHN.

entries.label: Assassination of Calvo-Sotelo — the coup's trigger
entries.hint: The monarchist opposition leader José Calvo Sotelo is arrested at his home on 13 July 1936 by assault guards and murdered — in retaliation for the killing of lieutenant José Castillo; his body is dumped at the Madrid morgue; the assassination is the immediate pretext the conspirators use to justify the military uprising four days later.
entries.note: Calvo-Sotelo's murder 13 Jul 1936: documented in police and judicial records (AHN). The retaliatory logic: the socialist lieutenant Castillo had been murdered the day before — documented. Calvo-Sotelo's famous last Cortes speech (16 Jun 1936) predicting the violence: Diario de Sesiones. Preston (1994), Franco, ch.9: the standard account. The coup timetable: the conspirators accelerate their plans after the murder — documented in Mola's correspondence (Archivo General Militar).

entries.label: Bombing of Guernica — 26 April 1937
entries.hint: The Condor Legion and Italian Air Force bomb the Basque market town of Guernica for three hours on a busy market day — the number of dead is disputed (126–1,654 in sources) but the symbolic impact is immediate and global; Picasso begins his painting within days.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Wolfram von Richthofen diary entries, 18 and 23 April 1937: preserved in German military archives (Bundesarchiv-Militärarchiv) — describe the operation planning. Eyewitness: Father Alberto Onaindía (Basque priest, present that day) — his testimony published 1937. George Steer, The Times of London, 28 Apr 1937 (journalist, arrived hours after bombing). Casualty dispute: Basque government reported 1,654; local historians revised to 126–153. The uncertainty must be flagged.

entries.label: Battle of the Ebro — final Republican offensive
entries.hint: The largest battle of the Civil War — the Republic crosses the Ebro with 80,000 troops in an attempt to reconnect the two halves of Republican territory — the Nationalists had split Republican Spain in April 1938 by reaching the Mediterranean at Vinaròs, isolating Catalonia; relieving pressure on Valencia was a secondary objective; initial success is reversed over four months; the Republic loses 70,000 casualties it cannot replace.
entries.note: Republican military records (fragmentary): Archivo General Militar de Ávila. International Brigades records: ALBA digital archive (UCSD). Thomas (1961) ch.55–59. The battle as the Republic's last major offensive: Graham (2002), The Spanish Republic at War. International press coverage: New York Times archive, Le Monde archive.

entries.label: Assassination of Admiral Carrero Blanco — 1973
entries.hint: ETA plants a bomb under Carrero Blanco's car — the explosion launches the car over a five-storey building; Carrero Blanco was Franco's designated successor and the assassination changes the course of the Transition by removing the hardest-line Francoist from succession.
entries.note: Police and judicial records: AHN. Contemporary press: El País archive, ABC archive. The engineering of the tunnel: documented in ETA's own later accounts. Preston (2012), Franco's Caudillo, ch. final. The assassination's political impact — removing the most rigid Francoist from succession — is argued by Preston (1993) and Payne (1987) as inadvertently facilitating the Transition.

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: Constitutions, laws and the architecture of the Francoist state

entries.label: Constitution of the Second Republic — 1931
entries.hint: The 1931 Constitution separates Church and State, grants women the vote (the first time in Spanish history), establishes regional autonomy, and secularises education — it is the most progressive Spanish constitution to that date.
entries.note: Text: Gaceta de Madrid, 10 Dec 1931 (extant, digitised). Women's suffrage article 36: confirmed by vote in Cortes (debated by Clara Campoamor vs Victoria Kent — both women deputies). The Cortes debates on women's suffrage: Diario de Sesiones de las Cortes Constituyentes 1931. Arranz (2007), La Constitución de 1931.

entries.label: Statute of Autonomy of Catalonia — the Estatut
entries.hint: The first Statute of Catalan Autonomy, approved by referendum (592,961 votes to 3,276) and ratified by the Madrid Cortes — giving Catalonia its own government (Generalitat) and official status for the Catalan language; suppressed by Franco in 1939.
entries.note: Gaceta de Madrid, 21 Sep 1932: text of the Estatut. The referendum result: documented in the Archivo del Parlament de Catalunya. The Generalitat archives (ANC, Barcelona): primary repository. The Estatut's suppression by Franco: documented in BOE 1938–39. The 1932 statute became the template for all subsequent Catalan autonomy statutes.

entries.label: Franco abolishes Basque and Catalan autonomy (1937-1938)
entries.hint: By decree on 23 June 1937 Franco abolishes the Basque autonomy statute for Vizcaya and Guipuzcoa (calling them 'traitor provinces'); on 5 April 1938 he abolishes Catalan autonomy; Navarre and Alava retain their fueros for having supported the Nationalists; the legal destruction of regional autonomy is as central to Francoism as its political repression.
entries.note: Decree abolishing Basque autonomy 23 Jun 1937: BOE 24 Jun 1937 -- primary document. Decree abolishing Catalan Estatut 5 Apr 1938: BOE 6 Apr 1938. The exception for Navarre and Alava fueros: documented in the same BOE series. Payne (1987), The Franco Regime: the standard political history. The contrast with 1975-today folio (State of Autonomies, 1979): the reversal of this decree is documented there.

entries.label: Fuero del Trabajo — Francoist labour charter
entries.hint: Franco's labour charter — the first of the Leyes Fundamentales that together constitute the 'organic' Francoist state; it abolishes independent trade unions, bans strikes, and creates a corporatist 'vertical syndicate' system.
entries.note: Boletín Oficial del Estado (BOE), 10 Mar 1938: text of the Fuero del Trabajo. The six Leyes Fundamentales together: AHN documentation. Payne (1987), The Franco Regime 1936–1975: the standard academic analysis of the legal structure.

entries.label: Ley de Responsabilidades Políticas — retrospective repression
entries.hint: The Political Responsibilities Law holds all who 'opposed the National Movement' from October 1934 onwards legally liable — giving the regime a legal framework to confiscate property and imprison Republicans retroactively going back five years.
entries.note: BOE 13 Feb 1939: text of the law. The retroactivity to 1934 was historically unprecedented in Spanish law. Preston (1993), Franco, ch.20 on the repression's legal architecture. The law created 40,000+ proceedings: AHN Fondos del Tribunal Nacional de Responsabilidades Políticas.

entries.label: Concordat with the Vatican (1953) — legal foundation of National Catholicism
entries.hint: The Concordat of 27 August 1953 gives the Catholic Church enormous privileges in Spain — control of education at all levels, state funding, ecclesiastical courts, Catholic marriage as the only valid form; in exchange the Vatican legitimises Franco's regime internationally; it is the legal architecture of National Catholicism and arrives simultaneously with the US Pacts of Madrid.
entries.note: Concordat 27 Aug 1953: BOE 19 Oct 1953 -- primary document. Negotiated by Martín Artajo (Foreign Minister) and the Holy See: AGA (Ministerio de Asuntos Exteriores). The simultaneous US Pacts of Madrid (26 Sep 1953): the double diplomatic rehabilitation documented. Callahan (2000), The Catholic Church in Spain 1875-1998, ch.10: the standard study. The Concordat's education provisions: Article XXVI -- the Church controls Catholic schools. Partial renegotiation 1976: documented after the Transition.

entries.label: Fraga's Press Law (1966) — apertura and its limits
entries.hint: Manuel Fraga Iribarne's Ley de Prensa e Imprenta (18 March 1966) abolishes prior censorship and allows publications to operate without government pre-approval — a genuine but tightly bounded liberalisation; editors can now be prosecuted after publication (under the elastic concept of 'damage to national institutions'); it is the most significant legal relaxation of the Franco era and opens space for the later democratic press.
entries.note: Ley de Prensa e Imprenta 18 Mar 1966: BOE 19 Mar 1966 -- primary document. The 1938 Press Law it replaces: BOE 23 Apr 1938. Arias Ruiz (2003), La prensa espanola durante el franquismo: the standard study. The prosecutions under the 1966 law: AGA (Ministerio de Informacion y Turismo) -- dozens of editors fined or suspended. Seoane and Saiz (1996), Historia del periodismo en Espana, Vol.3: the standard press history. Cuadernos para el Dialogo (founded 1963 under the old law): the first opposition publication -- documented.

entries.label: Ley de Peligrosidad Social (1970) — targeting gay men and trans people
entries.hint: The Ley de Peligrosidad y Rehabilitación Social (1970) replaces the 1954 Ley de Vagos as the primary instrument of persecution — it specifically names homosexuals as a 'social danger'; gay men and trans people are sentenced to 'rehabilitation centres' (psychiatric facilities) or exile to specified towns; enforcement intensifies in the early 1970s.
entries.note: Ley de Peligrosidad y Rehabilitación Social (4 Aug 1970): BOE 6 Aug 1970 — primary document. The 'rehabilitation centres': Huelva and Badajoz facilities documented in AHN and press. Olmeda (2004), El látigo y la pluma: homosexuales en la España de Franco: the standard study, using AHN criminal records. The contrast with the Ley de Vagos (1954): the 1970 law specifically names homosexuality whereas the 1954 law used the category 'rufianes' (rogues). The law's enforcement statistics: AHN Audiencias Provinciales. Repeal: 1979, after the Transition.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: National Catholicism, anticlerical violence and the Church-State alliance

entries.label: Anticlerical violence — burning of churches 1931 and 1936
entries.hint: Churches and convents are burned in May 1931 (Madrid, Málaga) by Republican crowds and more extensively in July–September 1936 at Civil War's outbreak — approximately 7,000 clergy killed in Republican territory; documented in Church martyrology.
entries.note: The 1936 killings: documented in Causa General (1943) — a Francoist legal inquiry, biased but containing real data. Modern reassessment: Montero Moreno (1961), Historia de la persecución religiosa en España 1936–1939. The 7,000 figure: Callahan (2000), The Catholic Church in Spain. FLAG: the Causa General data requires critical handling as a Francoist propaganda document as well as a historical source.

entries.label: National Catholicism — Franco's ideological fusion
entries.hint: Franco fuses Catholic identity with Spanish nationalism — the Church receives extraordinary privileges (censorship power, education control, tax exemption) in exchange for legitimating the regime; the Concordat of 1953 formalises this arrangement.
entries.note: Concordat of 1953 (Spain-Vatican): text in BOE 19 Oct 1953. The National Catholicism ideology: analysed in Lannon (1987), Privilege, Persecution and Prophecy: The Catholic Church in Spain. The Church's educational role: documented in Education Ministry records (AHN). Pérez-Díaz (1993), The Return of Civil Society on the Church's post-Franco evolution.

entries.label: Opus Dei and the Technocrats — the Church that modernised Francoism
entries.hint: Opus Dei technocrats enter Franco's cabinet from 1957 — Laureano López Rodó, Mariano Navarro Rubio, Alberto Ullastres; their Plan de Estabilización (1959) and Development Plans abandon autarky and open Spain to foreign investment; the Spanish Miracle follows; Opus Dei simultaneously provides the regime's intellectual respectability and the foundation for its economic transformation.
entries.note: The cabinet reshuffle of February 1957: documented in the Boletín Oficial del Estado. López Rodó's memoirs: the primary insider account. The Plan de Estabilización (20 Jul 1959): BOE — primary document. Payne (1965), Franco's Spain: contextualises Opus Dei's political role. Preston (1993), Franco, ch.24: the standard account. The Matesa scandal (1969) — an Opus Dei financial fraud that fractures the technocrat coalition: documented in press (AGA, Archivo General de la Administración).

entries.label: Vatican II and the Church's break with Franco
entries.hint: Vatican II (1962–1965) forces a global rethinking of Church-state relations; in Spain, a younger generation of priests becomes active in the labour movement and Basque nationalism; the 1971 National Assembly of Bishops passes a motion asking forgiveness for the Church's role in the Civil War — it only narrowly fails the required two-thirds majority; the rupture between Franco and the Church is documented in the Vatican archives.
entries.note: Vatican II: Lumen Gentium and Gaudium et Spes (both 1964–1965) — the theological documents. The 1971 National Assembly of Bishops' vote: documented in the Assembly acts — the motion to ask forgiveness for taking sides in the Civil War received a majority but not the required two-thirds. The Añoveros Affair (1974 — Bishop of Bilbao arrested): AHN and Vatican archives. Callahan (2000), The Catholic Church in Spain 1875–1998, ch.12: the standard study. The Workers' Commissions (CCOO) and their priest networks: documented in Ministerio de Gobernación files.

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: Guernica, the Generation of '98, Lorca and Republican culture

entries.label: Federico García Lorca — poet, playwright, martyr
entries.hint: Lorca is shot by Nationalist forces in August 1936 near Granada — his body has never been found; his Romancero Gitano (1928) and Poeta en Nueva York (written 1929–30) are the most celebrated Spanish poems of the 20th century; his death became the defining symbol of Francoist cultural repression.
entries.note: Lorca shot 18 Aug 1936: documented in multiple contemporary accounts; Gibson (1983), Federico García Lorca: A Life, the standard biography. The location of his burial: subject of ongoing forensic investigation (mass grave near Víznar, Granada) — as of 2024 not conclusively located. Romancero Gitano first edition 1928: BNE. Poet in New York first edition 1940 (posthumous): BNE.

entries.label: Unamuno, Machado, Valle-Inclán — the Generation of '98 in full flower
entries.hint: The Generation of '98 produces its major works in the early 20th century — Unamuno's Del sentimiento trágico de la vida (1913), Machado's Campos de Castilla (1912), Valle-Inclán's esperpentos (from 1920); these works are primary sources for Spain's 20th-century intellectual crisis and feed directly into Republican cultural politics.
entries.note: Unamuno, Del sentimiento trágico de la vida (1913): BNE. Machado, Campos de Castilla (1912): BNE. Valle-Inclán, Luces de Bohemia (1920 — first esperpento): BNE. Note: the Generation of '98 as a movement is introduced in the spain-1808-1900 folio (t2); this entry covers the major works published in the 20th century. Fox (1997), Reimagining Spain: the standard English study.

entries.label: Flamenco and Republican culture — Carmen Amaya
entries.hint: Carmen Amaya, born in a shack by the Barcelona waterfront, becomes the greatest flamenco dancer of the 20th century — her 1936 performance tour is interrupted by the Civil War, she goes into exile in South America, returns under Franco; her career documents the survival of Romani-Andalusian culture across the rupture.
entries.note: Carmen Amaya's career: documented in contemporary press and film. Her 1963 film Amor de Dios (extant). Pohren (1964), The Art of Flamenco: primary documentation of her technique. The Romani community's experience under Francoism: Castro Varela & Dhawan (2020). Her passport and emigration records: Archivo General de la Administración (AGA), Alcalá de Henares.

entries.label: Buñuel and the Residencia de Estudiantes — surrealist cinema
entries.loc: Madrid (Residencia de Estudiantes); Paris; Mexico City
entries.hint: Luis Buñuel, Federico García Lorca and Salvador Dalí share rooms at the Residencia de Estudiantes in the 1920s — the intellectual crucible of Spain's Silver Age; Buñuel and Dalí co-write Un Chien Andalou (1929), the founding text of surrealist cinema; Buñuel's later Viridiana (1961) wins the Palme d'Or and is immediately banned in Spain.
entries.note: Un Chien Andalou (1929): directed Buñuel, written Buñuel and Dalí — BnF film archive. The Residencia de Estudiantes (founded 1910): Archivo de la Residencia de Estudiantes, Madrid — attendance records document Lorca, Dalí, Buñuel. Viridiana (1961): Palme d'Or Cannes — immediately banned in Spain by the Franco government; the censorship document is in AGA (Archivo General de la Administración). Edwards (1982), The Discreet Art of Luis Buñuel: the standard English study. The Residencia's role: Sánchez Vidal (1988), Buñuel, Lorca, Dalí: el enigma sin fin.

entries.label: Miguel Hernández — the goatherd poet who died in Franco's prison
entries.hint: Miguel Hernández, a self-taught goatherd from Orihuela, writes the defining poetry of the Civil War (Viento del pueblo, 1937) and the post-war imprisonment (Cancionero y romancero de ausencias, written in prison); his letters to his wife Josefina from prison are among the most moving primary documents of Francoist repression; he dies of tuberculosis in Alicante prison on 28 March 1942, aged 31.
entries.note: Cancionero y romancero de ausencias (written 1938–1941, published posthumously 1958): BNE. Prison letters to Josefina Manresa: published by her in 1974 — the primary documentary record of his imprisonment. Viento del pueblo (1937): BNE. His trial records: AHN — condemned to death, commuted to 30 years; died of tuberculosis. Cano Ballesta (1968), La poesía de Miguel Hernández: the standard study. UNESCO's proclamation of the Hernández centenary (2010): documents his global literary standing.

entries.label: Guernica — Picasso's response to the bombing
entries.hint: Picasso paints Guernica in six weeks after reading George Steer's newspaper report — 3.49m × 7.76m, oils on canvas; it becomes the defining image of 20th-century political art and the most discussed painting of the Spanish Civil War.
entries.note: Extant: Museo Reina Sofía, Madrid (since 1992, transferred from MoMA). Picasso began painting May 1937. The preparatory sketches (45 studies): also Reina Sofía. Van Hensbergen (2004), Guernica: The Biography of a Twentieth-Century Icon. The painting's political history — kept in MoMA throughout the Franco years at Picasso's explicit request — is itself a primary source on Spain-US relations.

entries.label: Valle de los Caídos — monument built by political prisoners
entries.loc: San Lorenzo de El Escorial, Madrid (extant)
entries.hint: Franco orders a vast underground basilica cut into a granite mountain using Republican political prisoners as forced labour — 558m tunnel, 150m cross visible for 30km; Franco himself is buried here until 2019 when his remains are exhumed and moved.
entries.note: Construction decree: BOE Apr 1940. The use of forced labour: documented in Sueiro (1976), El Valle de los Caídos. Franco's burial: documented 1975; exhumation 24 Oct 2019: BOE, Supreme Court ruling. Extant (closed since 2019, transformation ongoing). The monument is itself a primary source for Francoist ideology of 'reconciliation' through Catholic ritual.

entries.label: Paco de Lucía and Lola Flores — flamenco between tradition and modernity
entries.loc: Algeciras (Paco, born 1947); Jerez de la Frontera (Lola, born 1923)
entries.hint: Lola Flores ('La Faraona') is the defining popular artist of Franco's Spain — her copla and flamenco reach every household via radio and cinema; Paco de Lucía begins his professional career aged 11 and by 1967 records his first solo album; his technical revolution of flamenco guitar (incorporating jazz harmonics, picado speed) transforms the art form; both document flamenco's survival under the regime's appropriation of it as 'folk culture'.
entries.note: Lola Flores's first film El Negro que tiene tu negra (1956): filmoteca records. Her early radio career: documented in RTVE archive. Paco de Lucía's first album La fabulosa guitarra de Paco de Lucía (1967): Philips Records — the primary artefact. Caballero Bonald (1975), Luces y sombras del flamenco: the standard contemporary study (published the year of Franco's death). The regime's use of flamenco as 'Spain's authentic culture': documented in the Ministerio de Información y Turismo promotional materials (AGA).

entries.label: Joan Manuel Serrat and the Nova Cançó — singing resistance
entries.hint: Joan Manuel Serrat and the Nova Cançó movement (Raimon, Maria del Mar Bonet, Lluís Llach) use Catalan and Spanish song as anti-Franco resistance — Serrat's refusal to sing in Spanish at Eurovision 1968 (insisting on Catalan) is a political act documented in censorship files; his Mediterráneo (1971) becomes the most celebrated Spanish-language album of the century.
entries.note: Serrat's Eurovision refusal 1968: documented in RTVE and TVE censorship files (AGA, Sección Cultura). Mediterráneo (1971): CBS Records Spain — the original LP. The Nova Cançó movement: documented in Ministerio de Información y Turismo censorship records (AGA). Lluís Llach's L'estaca (1968): the primary anthem of anti-Franco Catalan resistance — censored immediately. Vidal Manzanares (2004), Joan Manuel Serrat: the standard biography. The censorship apparatus: the Junta de Censura de Actividades Teatrales — its files in AGA document each rejection.

entries.label: Dalí and Miró — Surrealism and the abstract from Catalonia
entries.hint: Salvador Dalí (The Persistence of Memory, 1931) and Joan Miró (Catalan Farm, 1921–1922) are alongside Picasso the three titans of 20th-century Spanish art — both Catalans, both shaped by the Residencia de Estudiantes, both internationally influential; Dalí at the Meadows Museum (Dallas) and Miró at the Fundació Joan Miró (Barcelona, 1975) anchor their legacies in Spain.
entries.note: The Persistence of Memory (1931): Museum of Modern Art, New York (MoMA 162.1934). Catalan Farm (1921–1922): Museo Reina Sofía, Madrid. Dalí's friendship with Lorca and Buñuel at the Residencia: documented in correspondence (Fundació Gala-Salvador Dalí, Figueres). Miró's Fundació (designed by Josep Lluís Sert, 1975): Barcelona. Dalí Theatre-Museum, Figueres: opened 1974 (Dalí designed it himself — documented). Gibson (1997), The Shameful Life of Salvador Dalí: the standard biography.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Intellectuals, exile and the suppression of free thought

entries.label: Regeneracionismo — the intellectual diagnosis of Spain's crisis
entries.hint: The 1898 defeat triggers regeneracionismo — the intellectual movement diagnosing Spain's 'problem': Joaquin Costa's Oligarquia y caciquismo (1902) argues the state is captured by local boss networks; Lucas Mallada's Los males de la patria (1890) precedes it; the movement frames Spain as a backward nation requiring radical modernisation — the intellectual framing that shapes both the Republic and Francoism.
entries.note: Joaquin Costa, Oligarquia y caciquismo (1901): BNE -- primary document based on survey data. Lucas Mallada, Los males de la patria (1890): BNE. The famous encuesta (survey) Costa conducted among lawyers and notaries: AHN -- the empirical foundation of his argument. Fox (1997), Reimagining Spain: the standard modern study. Tuñon de Lara (1967), Medio siglo de cultura espanola: the standard historiographical survey. Costa's 'school, larder and seven keys for the tomb of El Cid' (his summary of what Spain needs): documented in multiple contemporary sources.

entries.label: Unamuno — pioneer of European existentialist philosophy
entries.hint: Miguel de Unamuno's Del sentimiento trágico de la vida (1912) explores the agonising conflict between rational scepticism and the desperate human desire for immortality — predating and directly influencing French existentialism; Sartre and Camus both cite him; Unamuno is the first philosopher in European tradition to place this 'tragic sense' at the centre of human existence.
entries.note: Del sentimiento trágico de la vida (1912): BNE. English translation Crawford Flitch (1921): the vehicle of its European influence. Sartre's acknowledgment of Unamuno: documented in interviews — though Sartre's direct citation is debated, the conceptual overlap is documented by Nozick (1971), Miguel de Unamuno: the standard English study. The Salamanca rectorship: documented — his famous 'Venceréis, pero no convenceréis' confrontation with Millán Astray (October 1936) is one of the most documented scenes in Spanish cultural history. Unamuno died under house arrest 31 Dec 1936.

entries.label: Ortega y Gasset — The Revolt of the Masses (1930)
entries.hint: José Ortega y Gasset's La Rebelión de las Masas is the most internationally influential Spanish philosophical text of the 20th century — a diagnosis of European mass society that sells 15,000 copies in its first year and is translated into 20 languages.
entries.note: First edition: Revista de Occidente, Madrid 1930: BNE. The Revista de Occidente (1923–1936) itself: key intellectual journal of the era, archive at BNE. Moran (1996), El maestro en el erial: Ortega y Gasset and the Second Republic. The book's international reception: documented in translation records and press reviews.

entries.label: Republican propaganda posters — a visual primary corpus
entries.hint: The Spanish Republic produces over 2,000 propaganda posters — lithographs by Renau, El Lissitzky-influenced designs by various artists — constituting the richest single corpus of political graphic design of the 20th century, now digitised by multiple archives.
entries.note: Brandeis University Spanish Civil War Collection: 5,000+ items. BNE digital collection: hundreds of posters. Cary (1973) on Republican poster art. The Nationalist counter-poster tradition: smaller and less artistically significant. Southworth Collection (UCSD): the most comprehensive private collection, now digitised. The posters as primary evidence for Republican ideology: Brihuega (1982).

entries.label: George Orwell — Homage to Catalonia (1938)
entries.hint: Orwell fights with the POUM militia on the Aragon front, witnesses the Communist-Anarchist street fighting in Barcelona (May 1937), is shot through the throat, and barely escapes — his eyewitness account is the most vivid English-language primary source for the Republic's internal conflicts.
entries.note: First edition: Secker & Warburg, London 1938 — British Library holds the first edition. Orwell's POUM militia card (extant): documents his service. His medical records from the bullet wound: documented in Orwell's own correspondence (University College London, Orwell Archive). Crick (1980), George Orwell: A Life. The book sold only 638 copies in Orwell's lifetime.

entries.label: María Zambrano — razón poética and the philosophy of exile
entries.loc: Madrid (born; ILE educated); Rome; Havana; Paris; Geneva (exile)
entries.hint: María Zambrano (1904–1991) formulates razón poética (poetic reason) as an alternative to pure Cartesian rationalism — arguing that poetry, myth and deep contemplation reveal truths inaccessible to cold logic; exiled after the Civil War, she develops her philosophy across 45 years of exile and receives the Cervantes Prize (1988) and Prince of Asturias Award (1981) after the Transition.
entries.note: Filosofía y Poesía (1939): first published in Mexico — the foundational text of razón poética. El hombre y lo divino (1955): the mature statement. Hacia un saber sobre el alma (1950). Cerezo Galán (2011), María Zambrano: pensadora de la Aurora: the standard Spanish study. The Cervantes Prize (1988): the highest honour in Spanish-language literature. Her return to Spain 1984: documented — she was the last major Republican exile to return. Her ILE formation: documented in the Residencia de Estudiantes records.

entries.label: Exiled intellectuals — the other Spain in Paris and Mexico City
entries.loc: Paris (Leon Felipe; Rafael Alberti); Mexico City (Max Aub; Francisco Ayala)
entries.hint: The Republican exile produces one of the most remarkable concentrations of intellectual talent in 20th-century history — Leon Felipe's poetry of exile, Max Aub's six-novel Laberinto Magico cycle (the most complete literary account of the Civil War), Francisco Ayala's sociology, Maria Zambrano's philosophy; they constitute the 'other Spain' whose work Franco's regime systematically suppressed.
entries.note: Max Aub, El laberinto magico (6 vols, 1943-1968): Fondo de Cultura Economica (Mexico) -- primary. Leon Felipe, Llamadme publicano (1950): the exile's self-definition. Francisco Ayala, Recuerdos y olvidos (1982): memoir -- primary. The Junta de Cultura Espanola (Paris, 1939): its records held in the Archivo de la Segunda Republica en el Exilio (Salamanca). Caudet (1992), El exilio republicano de 1939: the standard study. Their works: banned in Spain until the Transition -- documented in AGA censorship records.

categories.label: Economy
categories.subtitle: Economy from colonial collapse to autarky to technocratic growth

entries.label: Post-1898 economic nationalism — the regeneracionismo movement
entries.hint: The 1898 colonial disaster (covered in the spain-1808-1900 folio) triggers a decade of economic nationalism — the regeneracionismo movement demands industrial modernisation, agricultural reform and education; Joaquín Costa's Oligarquía y caciquismo (1901) is its primary document; Barcelona's industrial economy accelerates while Castile's agrarian economy stagnates.
entries.note: Joaquín Costa, Oligarquía y caciquismo (1901): BNE — a primary document of the regeneracionismo movement based on surveys of local conditions. Costa's 'school, larder and seven keys for the tomb of El Cid': his famous summary of what Spain needs. The Catalan industrial acceleration after 1898: documented in INE statistics. Harrison (1978), An Economic History of Modern Spain: the standard study. Note: the 1898 military defeat itself is covered in spain-1808-1900 folio (entry c4).

entries.label: Telefonica (1924) — the first national telephone network
entries.hint: The Compania Telefonica Nacional de Espana is founded on 25 August 1924 under Primo de Rivera — an ITT monopoly concession; Spain's first national telecommunications infrastructure; by 1929 Madrid's Telefonica building on Gran Via is the tallest building in Europe; the network reaches 350,000 lines by 1931.
entries.note: Concession contract 25 Aug 1924: AHN. The Gran Via building (1929): extant. Calvo (1998), Historia de Telefonica 1924-1975: the standard corporate history. The Republican nationalisation attempt (1931): Congreso de los Diputados records. ITT's role documented in Agar (2003), The Government Machine.

entries.label: Francoist autarky — the hungry decade
entries.hint: Franco's autarky policy (economic self-sufficiency) produces a decade of stagnation and hunger — rationing cards are in use until 1952; Spain's GDP does not recover to 1935 levels until 1951; the black market (estraperlo) becomes pervasive.
entries.note: Rationing records: AGA, Alcalá de Henares. Ration cards (extant objects): multiple museum collections. GDP data: Prados de la Escosura (2003), El progreso económico de España. The estraperlo (black market): documented in police and court records. Viñas et al. (1979), Política comercial exterior en España 1931–1975: the standard economic history.

entries.label: INI (1941) — Francoist autarky's industrial engine
entries.hint: The Instituto Nacional de Industria (INI, founded 1941) is the central economic instrument of Francoist autarky — a massive state holding company modelled on Mussolini's IRI, designed to build self-sufficient industrial capacity; it creates highly protected, inefficient state monopolies (SEAT cars, RENFE railways, IBERIA airlines, ENDESA electricity) that dominate the economy until the 1959 Stabilisation Plan forces course correction.
entries.note: INI founding decree 25 Sep 1941: BOE. Sudrià (1999), 'La industrialización en España': the standard economic study. The IRI model: explicitly documented in INI's founding documents. Comín (1991), Historia de la empresa pública en España: the standard institutional study. The INI's companies: SEAT (1950), ENDESA (1944), IBERIA (nationalised 1943), ENSIDESA steel — all documented. The 1959 Plan de Estabilización as the INI's partial dismantling: see existing entry s3.

entries.label: RENFE (1941) — nationalising the railways
entries.hint: Franco nationalises the railway network into RENFE on 24 January 1941 — inheriting the pre-war wide-gauge system (1,668mm vs European 1,435mm standard) whose origins lie in the 1855 Railway Law; autarky leaves RENFE chronically underfunded; the gauge problem physically isolates Spain from Europe until the AVE era.
entries.note: RENFE founding decree 24 Jan 1941: BOE. Munoz Rubio (1995), RENFE: una historia social y economica: the standard study. The gauge decision origins: Comision de Ingenieros 1844 -- AHN. Pre-nationalisation chaos: 13 private companies, incompatible rolling stock -- Ministerio de Obras Publicas reports. Gomez-Mendoza (1989), Ferrocarril, industria y mercado en la modernizacion de Espana.

entries.label: SEAT 600 (1957) — the car that motorises Spain
entries.hint: The SEAT 600 (launched June 1957) is the car that motorises Spain — priced at 65,000 pesetas on instalment plan for the first time; 800,000 units sold; it is simultaneously the most visible symbol of the Spanish Miracle and of Francoist consumer society; what the Model T was to the US, the 600 is to Spain.
entries.note: SEAT 600 launch June 1957: ABC 13 Jun 1957 (BNE Hemeroteca). Production records: Arxiu Nacional de Catalunya (SEAT corporate archive). SEAT founded 1950 as INI-Fiat joint venture: INI records (AHN). Gomez Mendoza (2000), De mitos y milagros: el INI 1941-1963: the standard study. Miguel (1975), La sociedad espanola 1975-1976: contemporary documentation of the 600's social meaning.

entries.label: Opus Dei technocrats and the Plan de Estabilización (1959)
entries.hint: Franco appoints Opus Dei-linked technocrats who impose austerity and open Spain to foreign investment — the 1959 Stabilisation Plan ends autarky and triggers the 'Spanish miracle' of 6% annual growth in the 1960s.
entries.note: Plan de Estabilización decree: BOE, Jul 1959. World Bank and IMF involvement: documented in their archives (Washington DC). Payne (1987), The Franco Regime, ch.17 on the technocrats. GDP growth 1960–1973: Prados de la Escosura (2003). The plan's social consequences — mass internal migration from Andalusia and Extremadura to Catalonia and Basque Country — documented in census records.

entries.label: The Spanish Miracle (1959–1973) — fastest growth in Western Europe
entries.loc: Spain (Catalonia; Basque Country; Madrid — industrial poles)
entries.hint: Following the 1959 Stabilisation Plan, Spain achieves the fastest sustained economic growth in Western Europe — GDP grows at c.7% annually for 14 years; mass industrialisation, rural-to-urban migration (5 million internal migrants), tourist boom (from 6 million tourists in 1960 to 34 million in 1973), and remittances from Spanish workers in France and Germany transform the country.
entries.note: GDP growth data (1959–1973): Banco de España historical series and OECD. The internal migration figures: INE census 1960 and 1970. Tourist arrivals: INE tourism statistics. Harrison (1985), The Spanish Economy in the Twentieth Century, ch.8: the standard study. Tortella (2000), The Development of Modern Spain, ch.10: contextualises the miracle's structural limits. The contrast with the autarky period (1939–1959): documented in the same INE series. The miracle ends at the 1973 oil shock: documented.

entries.label: Ramon y Cajal — the father of neuroscience (Nobel 1906)
entries.hint: Santiago Ramon y Cajal wins the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1906 — Spain's first Nobel — for proving the nervous system is composed of discrete individual cells (the neuron doctrine); his ink drawings of neural architecture are simultaneously scientific instruments and works of art; he founds modern neuroscience.
entries.note: Nobel Prize citation 1906: Nobel Foundation records. Cajal's Textura del sistema nervioso del hombre y de los vertebrados (1899-1904): Instituto Cajal, Madrid holds the original drawings (over 2,900 surviving). UNESCO Memory of the World for the drawing collection (2017). DeFelipe and Jones (1988), Cajal on the Cerebral Cortex: the standard English edition. Cajal, Recuerdos de mi vida (1917): his autobiography -- primary source.

entries.label: Torres Quevedo — chess automaton and the first analogue computer
entries.hint: Leonardo Torres Quevedo builds El Ajedrecista (1912) — the world's first chess-playing automaton, executing king-and-rook vs king endgames; builds the Niagara Falls aerial tramway (1916, still operating); and in 1920 the world's first analogue electromechanical computer; the most internationally significant Spanish engineer of the 20th century.
entries.note: El Ajedrecista (1912): demonstrated Paris Academy of Sciences; device survives at ETSICCP Madrid. Torres Quevedo (1912), Revista de Obras Publicas: primary description (BNE). The Niagara Falls Spanish Aero Car (1916): extant and operating. His 1920 electromechanical machine: Torres Quevedo (1920), Ensayo sobre Automatica, Real Academia de Ciencias. Randell (1982), From Analytical Engine to Electronic Digital Computer: places Torres Quevedo in computing history.

entries.label: Juan de la Cierva — the autogyro, precursor of the helicopter
entries.hint: Juan de la Cierva achieves the first successful autogyro flight on 9 January 1923 — a rotating-wing aircraft that cannot stall; the direct technological precursor of the helicopter; licensed internationally and manufactured in Britain, the US and France; Cierva dies in a conventional aircraft crash at Croydon in 1936.
entries.note: First autogyro flight 9 Jan 1923: ABC 10 Jan 1923 (BNE Hemeroteca). British patent UK 155,928 (1921). Cierva and Rose (1931), Wings of Tomorrow: primary English-language account. The C.30A autogyro (1934): manufactured by Avro, used by the RAF. Brooks (1988), Cierva Autogiros: the standard technical study. Sikorsky explicitly credited Cierva's work in developing the helicopter -- documented.

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: International intervention, exile and Cold War diplomacy

entries.label: Non-Intervention Agreement — European powers abandon the Republic
entries.hint: 27 European states sign the Non-Intervention Agreement while Germany and Italy openly supply Franco — the most cynical diplomatic exercise of the interwar period; the Republic receives Soviet aid but at a political cost (Soviet interference in Republican politics).
entries.note: Non-Intervention Committee records: National Archives (UK), FO 849 series. German military aid (Operation Magic Fire): Bundesarchiv-Militärarchiv. Italian aid: ACS (Rome). Soviet aid: RGVA (Moscow), partly published in Radosh et al. (2001), Spain Betrayed. Thomas (1961) ch.25–28 synthesises the international dimension.

entries.label: Republican exile — 500,000 flee to France (1939)
entries.loc: Pyrenean border crossings; Argelès-sur-Mer (French internment camps)
entries.hint: Half a million Republicans flee to France in the Retirada (February–March 1939) — the largest refugee movement in European history to that date; they are interned in camps on the beaches; many go on to Mexico, which receives the Republican government in exile.
entries.note: French government records: Archives nationales (Paris), series F7 (refugees). The Retirada's scale: documented in French military and police records. Mexican reception: Archivo Histórico Diplomático Mexicano. Pons Prades (1977), Republicanos españoles en la Segunda Guerra Mundial. Martínez (2007) on the Republican exile community.

entries.label: Republican exile in Mexico and Latin America — the other Spain
entries.loc: Mexico City; Buenos Aires; Havana; Toulouse
entries.hint: 500,000 Republicans cross into France (1939); approximately 25,000 reach Mexico under President Cardenas's asylum offer; the exiled Spanish Republic maintains a government-in-exile in Mexico City until 1977; the exile community creates universities, publishing houses and cultural institutions; Mexican Spanish absorbs a generation of Spanish intellectuals including Leon Felipe, Max Aub, Francisco Ayala and Luis Bunuel.
entries.note: Mexico's asylum offer: President Cardenas's decree documented in Mexican Foreign Ministry records. The Spanish Republic in exile (Mexico City 1939-1977): its records held at the Archivo Historico del Partido Accion Nacional (Mexico). Fondo de Cultura Economica: founded with Republican exile involvement -- documented. Aznar (1969), La guerra espanola en la literatura mexicana: the standard study. The Mexican government's refusal to recognise Franco (maintained until 1977): Mexican Foreign Ministry records.

entries.label: Meeting at Hendaye (1940) — Franco frustrates Hitler
entries.hint: Franco meets Hitler at Hendaye on 23 October 1940 — Hitler demands Spain's entry into the war; Franco makes impossible demands (vast French colonial territories in North Africa, massive grain shipments) and refuses to commit; Hitler famously says he would rather have three teeth pulled than negotiate with Franco again; Spain remains non-belligerent.
entries.note: The Hendaye meeting 23 Oct 1940: German Foreign Ministry records (Auswärtiges Amt, Berlin) and Spanish Foreign Ministry records (AMAE). Hitler's 'teeth' remark: documented in Paul Schmidt's account (Hitler's interpreter, present at the meeting) — Schmidt (1949), Statist auf diplomatischer Bühne. Preston (1993), Franco: the standard English biography, ch.18: the most detailed account using both German and Spanish archives. Franco's demands: cession of French Morocco, Oran, and the Canaries — documented. The meeting's outcome: Hitler writes to Mussolini that dealing with Franco was impossible.

entries.label: US-Spain Pacts of Madrid (1953) — Cold War legitimacy
entries.hint: The United States signs the Pacts of Madrid with Franco — giving the US military bases in Spain in exchange for economic aid and diplomatic recognition; the pacts legitimise the dictatorship internationally and mark Spain's integration into Cold War Western structures.
entries.note: Pacts of Madrid (Mutual Defence Assistance Agreement, 26 Sep 1953): US National Archives (NARA), State Department records. Declassified US diplomatic cables on the negotiation: FRUS 1952–54 Vol.VI. Viñas (2003), En las garras del águila: the standard Spanish study. The simultaneous Concordat with the Vatican (July 1953): the two agreements together define the Francoist international settlement.

entries.label: Mass tourism arrives — 2 million (1955) to 34 million (1973)
entries.loc: Costa del Sol; Costa Brava; Balearic Islands (main destinations)
entries.hint: Mass foreign tourism transforms Spain from 1955 — 2 million visitors in 1955, 6 million in 1960, 34 million by 1973; the arrivals are primarily northern European (Swedish, German, British); they bring foreign exchange funding the Miracle and cultural liberalisation that bypasses censorship; the bikini on Spanish beaches is a political fact documented in Ministerio de Informacion y Turismo files.
entries.note: Tourism statistics: INE and Ministerio de Informacion y Turismo annual reports -- primary data. The bikini controversy: AGA (Ministerio de Informacion y Turismo) -- documented instructions to Guardia Civil. Barke and France (1996), Tourism in Spain: Critical Issues: the standard English study. Pack (2006), Tourism and Dictatorship: uses the AGA archive to document how tourism was politically managed. The exchange dimension: peseta earnings from tourism documented in Banco de Espana balance of payments reports.

entries.label: Spanish workers in Europe — 1 million emigrants fuel the Miracle
entries.hint: Over 1 million Spaniards work in France, Germany and Switzerland by 1965 — escaping rural poverty and funding Spain's consumer revolution with their remittances; the remittances are the second largest source of foreign exchange after tourism; the workers return with European democratic expectations that the regime cannot entirely suppress; the migration is the human face of the Spanish Miracle.
entries.note: Emigration statistics: IEM (Instituto Espanol de Emigracion) annual reports -- primary data. Remittance figures: Banco de Espana balance of payments. The bilateral labour agreements (Spain-Germany 1960, Spain-France 1961, Spain-Switzerland 1961): AGA (Ministerio de Trabajo). Babiano and Fernandez Asperilla (1999), La patria en la maleta: the standard study using IEM records. The cultural return effect: documented in oral history projects and in the Ministerio de Gobernacion surveillance reports (AGA).

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: Spain in its 20th-century European and global context

entries.label: World War I — Spain neutral, economically transformed
entries.hint: Spain remains neutral throughout WWI — the last major European war in which it takes no part; neutrality brings an economic windfall (Spanish exports to both sides boom) but also social disruption; the war accelerates industrialisation in Catalonia and the Basque Country, deepens regional inequality, and radicalises the labour movement; the post-war crisis of 1917–1923 flows directly from wartime strains.
entries.note: Spain's formal neutrality declaration August 1914: Gaceta de Madrid. The export boom: Banco de Espana statistics 1914-1918 -- documented. The 1917 crisis (general strike + military juntas + Catalan assembly simultaneously): AHN and press (La Vanguardia, ABC -- BNE Hemeroteca). Ben-Ami (1983), The Origins of the Second Republic in Spain: analyses WWI's role in destabilising the Restoration system. Harrison (1985), The Spanish Economy in the Twentieth Century, ch.4: quantifies the wartime economic transformation.

entries.label: Disaster of Annual (1921) — Morocco annihilates a Spanish army
entries.hint: Moroccan Rif fighters under Abd el-Krim annihilate a Spanish army of 13,000 soldiers at Annual on 22 July 1921 — the worst Spanish military defeat in a century; the disaster shatters Spain's imperial prestige, triggers a massive parliamentary crisis (the Picasso Report investigation), and leads directly to Primo de Rivera's coup of September 1923.
entries.note: The Battle of Annual 22 Jul 1921: documented in Spanish military records (Archivo General Militar, Ávila). Spanish casualties: c.12,000–13,000 dead (exact figure disputed) — confirmed in parliamentary commission. The Picasso Report: Comisión Picasso investigation 1921–1923 — documented in Congreso de los Diputados. The connection to Primo de Rivera's coup: explicit in contemporary press and in Ben-Ami (1983). Abd el-Krim's subsequent Rif Republic (1921–1926): documented. Shannon Fleming (1991), Primo de Rivera and Abd-el-Krim: the standard English study.

entries.label: League of Nations and the Republic's failed appeal (1936-1937)
entries.hint: The Spanish Republic appeals repeatedly to the League of Nations against Non-Intervention — Foreign Minister Alvarez del Vayo's speeches at Geneva document the international abandonment of a legitimate elected government; the League debates but takes no action; the failure is a primary case study in the collapse of collective security in the 1930s.
entries.note: Alvarez del Vayo's League speeches (Sep 1936, Sep 1937): League of Nations Official Journal Special Supplement -- primary documents. The Non-Intervention Committee records: National Archives (UK), FO 849 series. Thomas (1961), The Spanish Civil War, Appendix III: reproduces the key diplomatic exchanges. Alpert (1994), A New International History of the Spanish Civil War: the standard diplomatic study. The League's inaction: documented in the Official Journal -- no binding resolution ever passed on Spain.

entries.label: Spain's neutrality in World War II
entries.hint: Franco navigates WWII through deliberate diplomatic shifts: 'non-belligerent' (June 1940 — after France's fall, showing open Axis support short of military entry), sending the Blue Division to the Eastern Front; under Allied economic pressure and changing Axis fortunes, Spain reverts to formal 'neutrality' in October 1943; the terminological distinction is a primary diplomatic fact.
entries.note: Blue Division records: Archivo General Militar de Ávila; German Bundesarchiv-Militärarchiv. The 47,000 volunteers: documented in enlistment records. Kleinfeld & Tambs (1979), Hitler's Spanish Legion: the standard study. The neutrality negotiations: US NARA, State Department decimal files on Spain. Franco's Hendaye meeting with Hitler (23 Oct 1940): German Foreign Ministry records (PAAA, Berlin).

entries.label: UN ostracism and rehabilitation of Franco's Spain
entries.hint: The 1946 UN resolution recommends member states withdraw ambassadors from Spain (45 to 2 vote) — Spain is isolated; by 1955 the Cold War reverses this and Spain is admitted to the UN; the complete diplomatic rehabilitation of a Fascist regime by Western powers.
entries.note: UN Resolution 39(I), 12 Dec 1946: UN General Assembly records (digitised). The ambassador withdrawals: documented in diplomatic correspondence (AHN). Spain's UN admission 14 Dec 1955: UN records. The US role in rehabilitation: FRUS 1952–54 Vol.VI. Payne (1987), The Franco Regime, ch.16 on the Cold War transformation.

entries.label: UN admission (1955) — Franco's Spain rehabilitated
entries.hint: Spain is admitted to the United Nations on 14 December 1955 as part of a Cold War package deal (16 states admitted simultaneously) — ending the UN ostracism declared in 1946; the US had already bilaterally rehabilitated Franco through the Pacts of Madrid (1953); the UN admission is the formal completion of Franco's international reintegration.
entries.note: UN General Assembly Resolution 995(X) 14 Dec 1955: UN records. The 1946 ostracism: UNGA Resolution 39(I) 12 Dec 1946 -- recommending member states withdraw ambassadors. The US bilateral rehabilitation (Pacts of Madrid, 26 Sep 1953): documented in exchange entry ex3. Portero (1989), Franco aislado: the standard study of the ostracism period. The package deal: 16 states simultaneously admitted -- documented as a Cold War compromise between US and Soviet blocs.

entries.label: Green March — Spain abandons the Spanish Sahara (1975)
entries.hint: On 6 November 1975 — while Franco is dying — Hassan II of Morocco sends 350,000 civilians across the border into Spanish Sahara in the 'Green March'; Spain, under Arias Navarro's caretaker government, signs the Madrid Accords (14 November 1975) surrendering the territory to Morocco and Mauritania; the Sahrawi population becomes stateless; the conflict remains unresolved.
entries.note: The Green March 6 Nov 1975: UN Security Council Resolution 380 (6 Nov 1975) — condemning the march. The Madrid Accords 14 Nov 1975: AGA (Ministerio de la Presidencia); Morocco and Mauritania. Hodges (1983), Western Sahara: The Roots of a Desert War: the standard study. The ICJ Advisory Opinion (16 Oct 1975): found no territorial sovereignty — ignored by Morocco. The Sahrawi refugees: documented in UNHCR reports from 1976. Franco's death (20 Nov 1975): five days after the Madrid Accords.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored · including categorical shifts

entries.label: Clara Campoamor — architect of women's suffrage
entries.hint: Clara Campoamor argues for women's suffrage in the 1931 Constituent Cortes against opposition from fellow Republican Victoria Kent — her speech on 1 October 1931 is the primary document of Spanish feminism; women vote for the first time in the November 1933 elections.
entries.note: Campoamor's speech, 1 Oct 1931: Diario de Sesiones de las Cortes Constituyentes, 1 Oct 1931 (digitised, Congreso de los Diputados archive). The vote: Article 36 of the 1931 Constitution. Campoamor (1936), El voto femenino y yo: her own memoir of the suffrage campaign, BNE. Nash (1991), Defying Male Civilization: Women in the Spanish Civil War.

entries.label: Seccion Femenina — Francoist women's organisation and its paradoxes
entries.hint: The Seccion Femenina (founded 1934 by Pilar Primo de Rivera) trains over 3 million women in domestic arts, physical education and Francoist ideology — but simultaneously creates the only legitimate female public space in the regime, training physical education teachers, nurses and rural extension workers; its paradox is that it professionalises women in the name of domesticity.
entries.note: Seccion Femenina records: AGA (Cultura) -- the primary archive. Pilar Primo de Rivera's memoirs: Recuerdos de una vida (1983). Richmond (2003), Women and Spanish Fascism: The Women's Section of the Falange 1934-1959: the standard English study using AGA records. The 3 million trained: documented in Seccion Femenina annual reports (AGA). The physical education teachers: the only female professional group permitted to travel and work publicly under early Francoism -- documented.

entries.label: Mujeres Libres — anarchist women's organisation 1936–1939
entries.hint: Mujeres Libres (Free Women) organises 20,000 members during the Civil War — running schools, childcare centres and training programmes; their magazine and archive is the primary source for women's autonomous political organising in the Spanish Republic.
entries.note: Mujeres Libres magazine (1936–1938): digitised, BNE and IISG Amsterdam. The organisation's records: International Institute of Social History (IISG), Amsterdam — the primary archive. Ackelsberg (1991), Free Women of Spain: Anarchism and the Struggle for the Emancipation of Women: the standard scholarly study. Membership figure of 20,000: documented in the organisation's own publications.

entries.label: Federica Montseny — first female minister in Western Europe (1936)
entries.hint: Federica Montseny is appointed Minister of Health on 4 November 1936 — the first woman to hold ministerial office in Western Europe; an anarchist leader of the CNT-FAI, she introduces Spain's first abortion law (1937) and establishes care homes for war orphans; she spends 37 years in French exile after 1939 and returns to Spain in 1977.
entries.note: Cabinet appointment 4 Nov 1936: Gaceta de la Republica 5 Nov 1936 -- primary document. The abortion decree (25 Dec 1936): Gaceta de la Republica -- the first legal abortion in Spanish history. Montseny's own memoir: Mis primeros cuarenta anos (1987): primary source. Nash (1995), Defying Male Civilization: Women in the Spanish Civil War: the standard study. Montseny's return to Spain 1977: documented in press. Her ministry lasted only months (until May 1937) but established legislative precedents.

entries.label: Forced labour under Francoism — Republican prisoners
entries.loc: Valle de los Caídos; Cuelgamuros; railway and canal construction sites
entries.hint: Franco uses Republican political prisoners as forced labour on construction projects including the Valle de los Caídos — an estimated 20,000 prisoners work at the site; the legal mechanism (Redención de Penas por el Trabajo) frames exploitation as 'redemption'.
entries.note: Redención de Penas por el Trabajo decree (1939): BOE. The Valle de los Caídos forced labour: documented in Sueiro (1976) and Aguilar (2008), Memory and Amnesia. Prisoner records: AHN. The number 20,000 at Valle de los Caídos: documented in Fernández-Poyatos (1995). This is a CATEGORICAL SHIFT: forced labour reframed as spiritual 'redemption' — one of the most cynical legislative manoeuvres of the dictatorship.

entries.label: Niños robados — babies stolen from Republican mothers
entries.hint: Beginning in Francoist women's prisons (1939), babies are systematically removed from Republican mothers under the pseudo-scientific guidance of psychiatrist Antonio Vallejo-Nájera, who claimed Marxism was a hereditary trait to be eradicated; the practice evolves into a lucrative church-backed illegal adoption network continuing into the 1980s–90s; estimated 30,000–300,000 children affected.
entries.note: Vallejo-Nájera's publications (1938–1939): 'Eugenesia de la Hispanidad' and 'Biopsiquismo del fanatismo marxista' — documented in military archives. The 2011 Parliamentary Commission: Congreso de los Diputados — the first official recognition. Armengou & Belis (2002), Els nens perduts del franquisme (TV documentary): the foundational journalistic investigation. Vinyes et al. (2002), Los niños perdidos del franquismo: the standard study. The Asociación Nacional para la Búsqueda de Niños Robados: documents individual cases ongoing. This is the most severe CATEGORICAL SHIFT in the folio: state-sanctioned removal of children from their legal mothers based on political identity.

entries.label: The Maquis — Republican guerrillas in Spain's mountains
entries.hint: After the 1939 defeat, thousands of Republican soldiers take to the mountains — the maquis wage guerrilla war against the Franco regime for over a decade; their lived reality is extreme isolation, survival in caves and farmhouse networks, and brutal counter-insurgency by the Civil Guard that includes torture and execution of civilian supporters (enlaces) who supply them.
entries.note: Maquis documentation: Archivo General de la Administración (AGA) and military records — Civil Guard reports on operations. Heine (1983), La oposición política al franquismo: the standard survey. Sánchez Agustí (2001), Maquis a Catalunya: uses regional archives. The Civil Guard's 'pacification' records: documented in AGA. The last active maquis (Francisco Sabaté Llopart, killed 1960): documented in press. The civilian network (enlaces): their torture and execution documented in trial records (Consejos de Guerra, AHN). A living memory: oral history projects document maquis survivors into the 2000s.

entries.label: Las Trece Rosas — thirteen women executed on 5 August 1939
entries.loc: Madrid (Cementerio del Este — executed at dawn)
entries.hint: Thirteen young women — the youngest aged 18, members of the Socialist Youth — are executed by firing squad at dawn on 5 August 1939; charged with 'military rebellion' for alleged involvement in a bomb plot; their letters written the night before execution to their families are among the most documented primary sources of Francoist terror against women.
entries.note: The trial records: AHN, Fondos Contemporáneos, Audiencia Territorial de Madrid — documented. The execution order: Archivo del Cementerio de la Almudena. The last letters (written the night of 4–5 August 1939): published in full by Ferrero (2003), Las Trece Rosas: the foundational modern account (based on AHN records). The youngest, Julia Conesa (18): documented. Preston (2012), The Spanish Holocaust, ch.17: uses the trial records. The Trece Rosas are the most documented case of gendered political execution in early Francoism.

entries.label: Anos del hambre — hunger and the black market under autarky
entries.loc: Spain (urban poor; rural landless; political prisoners worst affected)
entries.hint: The 1940s are 'los anos del hambre' — caloric intake in Spain falls below 1,000 calories per day in some provinces; rationing (until 1952) fails to prevent malnutrition; the black market (estraperlo) becomes the parallel economy; infant mortality rises; the UN Health Organisation's 1946 report documents Spain as among the most malnourished countries in Europe.
entries.note: WHO/UNRRA nutrition report on Spain (1946): UN archives. Municipal health records documenting malnutrition: Archivo Municipal de Madrid and Barcelona. Barciela (1981), La agricultura espanola y el franquismo: documents food production collapse. Richards (1998), A Time of Silence: uses provincial health records to document the anos del hambre. Ration books (cartillas de racionamiento): surviving examples in Museo de Historia de Madrid -- primary material culture. The estraperlo: prosecuted in over 500,000 cases -- Guardia Civil records.

entries.label: Francesc Boix — Spanish Republicans in Nazi concentration camps
entries.hint: Approximately 7,000 Spanish Republicans are deported to Nazi concentration camps, primarily Mauthausen; Francesc Boix, a Catalan photographer and prisoner, secretly preserves thousands of photographs documenting camp conditions and SS atrocities; his photographs are entered as evidence at the Nuremberg Trials (1945-1946) — the only Spaniard to testify at Nuremberg; he dies in Paris aged 30.
entries.note: Boix's Nuremberg testimony (29 Jan 1946): International Military Tribunal records (Avalon Project, Yale Law School) -- primary. His photographs: held at the Museu Nacional d'Art de Catalunya (MNAC) and the Amical de Mauthausen archive. The 7,000 Spanish deportees: documented in the Triangulo Azul project (Universidad de Salamanca). Pons Prades (1974), Republicanos espanoles en la Segunda Guerra Mundial: the first comprehensive study. Bermejo (2002), Francesc Boix, el fotografo de Mauthausen: the standard biography.

entries.label: Ley de Vagos y Maleantes — criminalisation of homosexuality
entries.hint: The 1933 Vagrancy Law is amended in 1954 to explicitly include homosexuals as subject to detention and forced labour camps — a CATEGORICAL SHIFT from implicit social stigma to explicit criminal category; replaced by the even harsher Ley de Peligrosidad Social (1970).
entries.note: BOE 17 Jul 1954: the amendment text. The Ley de Peligrosidad y Rehabilitación Social (1970): BOE 6 Aug 1970 — replaced the 1954 law with even more explicit criminalisation. Ugarte Pérez (2008), Una discriminación universal: la homosexualidad bajo el franquismo: the primary modern study. The internment camps (especially Badajoz): documented in prison records, partly in AGA. The transition to democracy formally decriminalised homosexuality in 1979.
